title: Ensure that the proxy file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the proxy file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that the proxy file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: The proxy file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the kube-proxy service in the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system.
input_row.audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/proxy Verify that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/proxy Impact: None Default Value: By default, proxy file has permissions of 644. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-proxy/   181 | P a g e
input_row.impact: None Default Value: By default, proxy file has permissions of 644.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control requires checking the file permissions of the kube-proxy configuration file (e.g., /etc/kubernetes/proxy) on each worker node. File permissions are an OS-level property and are not exposed via the Kubernetes API, kubectl, or any Kubernetes controller configuration. The audit instructions explicitly require running OS-level commands (e.g., stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/proxy) directly on the node. Therefore, this check cannot be automated via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl and requires manual/host-level access.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into each Kubernetes worker node.
2. Run the following command to check the permissions of the kube-proxy configuration file (replace the path if your proxy file is located elsewhere):
   ```
   stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/proxy
   ```
3. Verify that the output is 644 or a more restrictive value (e.g., 640, 600).
4. Confirm that the file is not world-writable and is only writable by the owner (typically root or an administrator).
5. Repeat for all worker nodes in the cluster.
6. If any file has permissions less restrictive than 644, remediate by running:
   ```
   chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/proxy
   ```
7. Document the results for compliance records.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES 1.6 BENCHMARK V1.0.0 ARCHIVE
